title: Chapter 2: Data Analysis With Python Projects - Quiz
description: Test your understanding of data analysis project design, statistical analysis, and visualization best practices

questions.question: What is the first step you should take when beginning a data analysis project?

options.text: Immediately begin building machine learning models
Understand the problem, define the questions you want to answer, and explore the dataset
Create visualizations before looking at the raw data
Remove all columns with any missing values

questions.explanation: The first step in any data analysis project is to clearly define the problem and the questions you want to answer, then explore the dataset to understand its structure, contents, and quality. This exploratory data analysis (EDA) phase guides all subsequent decisions about cleaning, transformation, and modeling.

questions.question: What is data wrangling?

options.text: The process of creating visualizations from clean data
The process of transforming and mapping raw data into a more useful format for analysis
The process of training machine learning models
The process of collecting data from web APIs

questions.explanation: Data wrangling (also called data munging) is the process of cleaning, structuring, and enriching raw data into a format suitable for analysis. This includes handling missing values, correcting data types, removing duplicates, normalizing values, and restructuring the data layout.

questions.question: What is the difference between the mean and the median of a dataset?

options.text: They are always the same value
The mean is the arithmetic average of all values, while the median is the middle value when data is sorted; the median is more robust to outliers
The mean is always larger than the median
The median is the most frequently occurring value, while the mean is the average

questions.explanation: The mean is calculated by summing all values and dividing by the count. The median is the middle value in a sorted dataset (or the average of two middle values for even-sized sets). The median is more robust to outliers — a single extreme value can dramatically shift the mean but not the median.

questions.question: When analyzing a dataset, why is it important to check for and handle outliers?

options.text: Outliers always represent data entry errors and must be deleted
Outliers have no effect on statistical analysis
Outliers can skew statistical measures like the mean and standard deviation, and may indicate data quality issues or genuinely unusual observations
Outliers only affect visualization and not numerical analysis

questions.explanation: Outliers can significantly affect statistical measures like the mean, standard deviation, and correlation coefficients. They may indicate data entry errors, measurement problems, or genuinely rare observations. It is important to investigate outliers to determine their cause before deciding whether to keep, transform, or remove them.

questions.question: Which type of chart is most appropriate for showing the distribution of a single continuous variable?

options.text: A pie chart
A histogram
A line chart
A stacked bar chart

questions.explanation: A histogram divides the range of a continuous variable into bins and shows the frequency (count) of observations in each bin, making it ideal for visualizing distributions. It reveals the shape (normal, skewed, bimodal), center, and spread of the data. Pie charts are for categorical proportions, and line charts are typically for trends over time.

questions.question: What does the correlation coefficient (r) measure?

options.text: The causal relationship between two variables
The strength and direction of the linear relationship between two variables, ranging from -1 to 1
The total variance in a dataset
The probability that one variable causes changes in another

questions.explanation: The Pearson correlation coefficient (r) measures the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two variables. Values range from -1 (perfect negative linear relationship) to +1 (perfect positive linear relationship), with 0 indicating no linear relationship. Importantly, correlation does not imply causation.

questions.question: When creating data visualizations, what is a best practice for choosing colors?

options.text: Use as many bright colors as possible to make the chart visually striking
Always use only black and white to keep charts professional
Use colorblind-friendly palettes and ensure colors convey meaningful distinctions in the data
Randomly assign colors to each data category

questions.explanation: Effective visualization uses color intentionally to communicate information. Using colorblind-friendly palettes (like those in seaborn or ColorBrewer) ensures accessibility. Colors should distinguish meaningful categories or values. Too many colors can overwhelm the viewer, and poor color choices can make charts unreadable for the ~8% of men with color vision deficiency.

questions.question: What is the purpose of normalizing or standardizing data before analysis?

options.text: To remove all outliers from the dataset
To bring different features to a comparable scale so that no single feature dominates due to its magnitude
To convert all categorical variables to numerical values
To increase the number of data points through interpolation

questions.explanation: Normalization (scaling to a range like 0-1) and standardization (scaling to zero mean and unit variance) ensure that features with different units and scales contribute equally to analysis and modeling. Without scaling, a feature like income (thousands) could dominate over age (tens) simply due to its larger magnitude.

questions.question: What does a box plot (box-and-whisker plot) display?

options.text: Only the mean and standard deviation of a dataset
The minimum, first quartile (Q1), median (Q2), third quartile (Q3), maximum, and potential outliers of a dataset
The frequency distribution of a categorical variable
The trend of a variable over time

questions.explanation: A box plot displays the five-number summary: minimum, Q1 (25th percentile), median (50th percentile), Q3 (75th percentile), and maximum. The box spans from Q1 to Q3 (the interquartile range), with a line at the median. Whiskers extend to the farthest data points within 1.5 × IQR, and points beyond that are plotted individually as potential outliers.

questions.question: Why is it important to split a dataset into training and testing sets when building predictive models?

options.text: To make the dataset smaller and faster to process
To evaluate the model's performance on unseen data and check for overfitting
To remove duplicate records from the dataset
To ensure the data is evenly distributed across categories

questions.explanation: Splitting data into training and testing sets allows you to evaluate how well a model generalizes to new, unseen data. If a model performs well on training data but poorly on test data, it is overfitting — memorizing the training data rather than learning general patterns. A common split is 80% training and 20% testing.
